title: Off-beat (music)

infobox.value: is the basic unit of time, the [pulse](/wiki/Pulse_(music) "Pulse (music)") (regularly repeating event), of the *mensural level*[[1]](#cite_note-1) (or *beat level*).[[2]](#cite_note-2) The beat is often defined as the rhythm listeners would tap their toes to when listening to a piece of music, or the numbers a musician [counts](/wiki/Counting_(music) "Counting (music)") while performing, though in practice this may be technically incorrect (often the first multiple level).[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] In popular use, *beat* can refer to a variety of related concepts, including [pulse](/wiki/Pulse_(music) "Pulse (music)"), [tempo](/wiki/Tempo "Tempo"), [meter](/wiki/Meter_(music) "Meter (music)"), specific [rhythms](/wiki/Rhythm "Rhythm"), and [groove](/wiki/Groove_(popular_music) "Groove (popular music)").

**8**). Divisions which require numbers, [tuplets](/wiki/Tuplet "Tuplet") (for example, dividing a [quarter note](/wiki/Quarter_note "Quarter note") into five equal parts), are irregular divisions and subdivisions. Subdivision begins two levels below the beat level: starting with a quarter note or a dotted quarter note, subdivision begins when the note is divided into sixteenth notes.

**4** time](/wiki/Quadruple_time "Quadruple time"), [counted](/wiki/Counting_(music) "Counting (music)") as "1 2 3 4, 1 2 3 4...", the first beat of the bar (downbeat) is usually the strongest [accent](/wiki/Accent_(music) "Accent (music)") in the melody and the likeliest place for a chord change, the third is the next strongest: these are "on" beats. The second and fourth are weaker—the "off-beats". Subdivisions (like eighth notes) that fall between the pulse beats are even weaker and these, if used frequently in a rhythm, can also make it "off-beat".[[9]](#cite_note-beat-9)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Off-beat (music)](/w/index.php?title=Off-beat_(music)&redirect=no "Off-beat (music)"))

Basic unit of time in music and music theory

For other uses, see [beat music](/wiki/Beat_music "Beat music"), [beat (acoustics)](/wiki/Beat_(acoustics) "Beat (acoustics)"), [drum beat](/wiki/Drum_beat "Drum beat"), and [beat (disambiguation)](/wiki/Beat_(disambiguation) "Beat (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Metric_levels.svg/500px-Metric_levels.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Metric_levels.svg)

Metric levels: beat level shown in middle with division levels above and multiple levels below.

In [music](/wiki/Music "Music") and [music theory](/wiki/Music_theory "Music theory"), the **beat** is the basic unit of time, the [pulse](/wiki/Pulse_(music) "Pulse (music)") (regularly repeating event), of the *mensural level*[[1]](#cite_note-1) (or *beat level*).[[2]](#cite_note-2) The beat is often defined as the rhythm listeners would tap their toes to when listening to a piece of music, or the numbers a musician [counts](/wiki/Counting_(music) "Counting (music)") while performing, though in practice this may be technically incorrect (often the first multiple level).[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] In popular use, *beat* can refer to a variety of related concepts, including [pulse](/wiki/Pulse_(music) "Pulse (music)"), [tempo](/wiki/Tempo "Tempo"), [meter](/wiki/Meter_(music) "Meter (music)"), specific [rhythms](/wiki/Rhythm "Rhythm"), and [groove](/wiki/Groove_(popular_music) "Groove (popular music)").

Rhythm in music is characterized by a repeating sequence of [stressed and unstressed beats](#On-beat_and_off-beat) (often called "strong" and "weak") and divided into [bars](/wiki/Bar_(music) "Bar (music)") organized by [time signature](/wiki/Time_signature "Time signature") and [tempo](/wiki/Tempo "Tempo") indications.

Beats are related to and distinguished from pulse, rhythm (grouping), and meter:

> Meter is the measurement of the number of pulses between more or less regularly recurring accents. Therefore, in order for meter to exist, some of the pulses in a series must be accented—marked for consciousness—relative to others. When pulses are thus counted within a metric context, they are referred to as *beats*.

— [Leonard B. Meyer](/wiki/Leonard_B._Meyer "Leonard B. Meyer") and [Cooper](/w/index.php?title=Grosvenor_Cooper&action=edit&redlink=1 "Grosvenor Cooper (page does not exist)") (1960)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

[Metric levels](/wiki/Meter_(music)#Metric_structure "Meter (music)") faster than the beat level are division levels, and slower levels are multiple levels. Beat has always been an important part of music. Some [music genres](/wiki/Music_genre "Music genre") such as [funk](/wiki/Funk "Funk") will in general de-emphasize the beat, while other such as [disco](/wiki/Disco "Disco") emphasize the beat to accompany dance.[[4]](#cite_note-hhd-4)

## Division

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Beat_(music)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Division")]

Main article: [Metre (music) § Metric structure](/wiki/Metre_(music)#Metric_structure "Metre (music)")

As beats are combined to form measures, each beat is divided into parts. The nature of this combination and division is what determines meter. Music where two beats are combined is in [duple meter](/wiki/Duple_meter "Duple meter"), music where three beats are combined is in [triple meter](/wiki/Triple_meter "Triple meter"). Music where the beat is split in two are in simple meter, music where the beat is split in three are called compound meter. Thus, simple duple (**2**  
**4**, **4**  
**4**, etc.), simple triple (**3**  
**4**), compound duple (**6**  
**8**), and compound triple (**9**  
**8**). Divisions which require numbers, [tuplets](/wiki/Tuplet "Tuplet") (for example, dividing a [quarter note](/wiki/Quarter_note "Quarter note") into five equal parts), are irregular divisions and subdivisions. Subdivision begins two levels below the beat level: starting with a quarter note or a dotted quarter note, subdivision begins when the note is divided into sixteenth notes.

## Downbeat and upbeat

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Beat_(music)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Downbeat and upbeat")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/6/65/Anacrusis-bwv736.png)](/wiki/File:Anacrusis-bwv736.png)

Beginning of [Bach's](/wiki/Johann_Sebastian_Bach "Johann Sebastian Bach") [BWV](/wiki/BWV "BWV") 736, with upbeat (anacrusis) in red. [Play](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/4/41/Anacrusis-bwv736.mid/Anacrusis-bwv736.mid.mp3 "Play audio")[ⓘ](/wiki/File:Anacrusis-bwv736.mid "File:Anacrusis-bwv736.mid")

The *downbeat* is the first beat of the [bar](/wiki/Bar_(music) "Bar (music)"), i.e. number 1. The *upbeat* is the last beat in the previous bar which immediately precedes, and hence anticipates, the downbeat.[[5]](#cite_note-Groveupbeat-5) Both terms correspond to the direction taken by the hand of a [conductor](/wiki/Conductor_(music) "Conductor (music)").

> This idea of directionality of beats is significant when you translate its effect on music. The crusis of a measure or a phrase is a beginning; it propels sound and energy forward, so the sound needs to lift and have forward motion to create a sense of direction. The anacrusis leads to the crusis, but doesn't have the same 'explosion' of sound; it serves as a preparation for the crusis.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

An anticipatory note or succession of notes occurring before the first barline of a piece is sometimes referred to as an upbeat figure, section or phrase. Alternative expressions include "pickup" and "[anacrusis](/wiki/Anacrusis "Anacrusis")" (the latter ultimately from Greek *ana* ["up towards"] and *krousis* ["strike"/"impact"] through French *anacrouse*). In English, *anákrousis* translates literally as "pushing up". The term anacrusis was borrowed from the field of [poetry](/wiki/Poetry "Poetry"), in which it refers to one or more unstressed extrametrical [syllables](/wiki/Syllable "Syllable") at the beginning of a line.[[5]](#cite_note-Groveupbeat-5)

## On-beat and off-beat

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Beat_(music)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: On-beat and off-beat")]

![\version "2.22.0"
\header { tagline = ##f}
\score {
  \drums \with {midiInstrument = "drums"}
  \with { \numericTimeSignature }
  {
    \repeat volta 2 {\stemUp
      r4 sne8 sne8 r4 sne r4 sne8 sne8 r4 sne \break
    }
  }
  \layout {}
}

\score {
  \unfoldRepeats {
    \drums \with {midiInstrument = "drums"}{
      \repeat volta 2 {
        r4 sne8 sne8 r4 sne r4 sne8 sne8 r4 sne
      }
    }
  }
  \midi { \tempo  4 = 100 }
}](//upload.wikimedia.org/score/l/6/l69lytkpq7se0ufokhe7dkhvsjdcocg/l69lytkp.png)

Off-beat or backbeat pattern, popular on snare drum[[7]](#cite_note-Schroedl-7)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Backbeat_chop.png/330px-Backbeat_chop.png)](/wiki/File:Backbeat_chop.png)

"[Skank](/wiki/Skank_(guitar) "Skank (guitar)")" guitar rhythm[[8]](#cite_note-8)[Play](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/9/98/Skank_on_Em64.mid/Skank_on_Em64.mid.mp3 "Play audio")[ⓘ](/wiki/File:Skank_on_Em64.mid "File:Skank on Em64.mid"). Often referred to as "upbeats", in parallel with [upstrokes](/wiki/Upstroke "Upstroke").

In typical Western music [**4**  
**4** time](/wiki/Quadruple_time "Quadruple time"), [counted](/wiki/Counting_(music) "Counting (music)") as "1 2 3 4, 1 2 3 4...", the first beat of the bar (downbeat) is usually the strongest [accent](/wiki/Accent_(music) "Accent (music)") in the melody and the likeliest place for a chord change, the third is the next strongest: these are "on" beats. The second and fourth are weaker—the "off-beats". Subdivisions (like eighth notes) that fall between the pulse beats are even weaker and these, if used frequently in a rhythm, can also make it "off-beat".[[9]](#cite_note-beat-9)

The effect can be easily simulated by evenly and repeatedly counting to four. As a background against which to compare these various rhythms a bass drum strike on the downbeat and a constant eighth note subdivision on ride cymbal have been added, which would be counted as follows (**bold** denotes a stressed beat):

* **1** 2 3 4 **1** 2 3 4 — [play eighth notes and bass drum alone](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/1/16/Backbeat_example_eighth_notes.mid/Backbeat_example_eighth_notes.mid.mp3 "Play audio")[ⓘ](/wiki/File:Backbeat_example_eighth_notes.mid "File:Backbeat example eighth notes.mid")
* **1** 2 **3** 4 **1** 2 **3** 4 — the stress here on the "on" beat [play](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/4/4c/Backbeat_example_unsyncopated.mid/Backbeat_example_unsyncopated.mid.mp3 "Play audio")[ⓘ](/wiki/File:Backbeat_example_unsyncopated.mid "File:Backbeat example unsyncopated.mid")

But one may syncopate that pattern and alternately stress the odd and even beats, respectively:

* 1 **2** 3 **4** 1 **2** 3 **4** — the stress on the "unexpected", or syncopated, beat [play](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/0/00/Backbeat_example.mid/Backbeat_example.mid.mp3 "Play audio")[ⓘ](/wiki/File:Backbeat_example.mid "File:Backbeat example.mid")

So "off-beat" is a musical term, commonly applied to [syncopation](/wiki/Syncopation "Syncopation"), that emphasizes the weak even beats of a bar, as opposed to the usual on-beat. This is a fundamental technique of [African polyrhythm](/wiki/African_polyrhythm "African polyrhythm") that transferred to popular western music. According to Grove Music, the "Offbeat is [often] where the downbeat is replaced by a rest or is tied over from the preceding bar".[[9]](#cite_note-beat-9) The downbeat can never be the off-beat because it is the strongest beat in **4**  
**4** time.[[10]](#cite_note-10) Certain genres tend to emphasize the off-beat, where this is a defining characteristic of rock'n'roll and [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska") music.

## Backbeat

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Beat_(music)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Backbeat")]

"Backbeat" redirects here. For other uses, see [Backbeat (disambiguation)](/wiki/Backbeat_(disambiguation) "Backbeat (disambiguation)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Backbeat_chop.png/330px-Backbeat_chop.png)](/wiki/File:Backbeat_chop.png)

Back beat[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) [Play](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/transcoded/b/b6/Backbeat_chop.mid/Backbeat_chop.mid.mp3 "Play audio")[ⓘ](/wiki/File:Backbeat_chop.mid "File:Backbeat chop.mid")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/16/Chuck_Berry51.JPG/250px-Chuck_Berry51.JPG)](/wiki/File:Chuck_Berry51.JPG)

"It's got a backbeat, you can't lose it" – [Chuck Berry](/wiki/Chuck_Berry "Chuck Berry"), "[Rock and Roll Music](/wiki/Rock_and_Roll_Music_(song) "Rock and Roll Music (song)")"

A **back beat**, or **backbeat**, is a [syncopated](/wiki/Syncopation "Syncopation") accentuation on the "off" beat. In a simple **4**  
**4** rhythm these are beats 2 and 4.[[13]](#cite_note-Grove-13)

"A big part of R&B's attraction had to do with the stompin' backbeats that make it so eminently danceable," according to the *Encyclopedia of Percussion*.[[14]](#cite_note-14) An early record with an emphasised back beat throughout was "[Good Rockin' Tonight](/wiki/Good_Rockin%27_Tonight "Good Rockin' Tonight")" by [Wynonie Harris](/wiki/Wynonie_Harris "Wynonie Harris") in 1948.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Although drummer [Earl Palmer](/wiki/Earl_Palmer "Earl Palmer") claimed the honor for "[The Fat Man](/wiki/The_Fat_Man_(song) "The Fat Man (song)")" by [Fats Domino](/wiki/Fats_Domino "Fats Domino") in 1949, which he played on, saying he adopted it from the final "shout" or "out" chorus common in [Dixieland](/wiki/Dixieland "Dixieland") jazz. There is a hand-clapping back beat on "[Roll 'Em Pete](/wiki/Roll_%27Em_Pete "Roll 'Em Pete")" by [Pete Johnson](/wiki/Pete_Johnson_(musician) "Pete Johnson (musician)") and [Big Joe Turner](/wiki/Big_Joe_Turner "Big Joe Turner"), recorded in 1938. A distinctive back beat can be heard on "Back Beat Boogie" by [Harry James](/wiki/Harry_James "Harry James") And His Orchestra, recorded in late 1939.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Other early recorded examples include the final verse of "Grand Slam" by [Benny Goodman](/wiki/Benny_Goodman "Benny Goodman") in 1942 and some sections of The [Glenn Miller Orchestra](/wiki/Glenn_Miller_Orchestra "Glenn Miller Orchestra")'s "(I've Got A Gal In) Kalamazoo", while [amateur direct-to-disc recordings](/wiki/Charlie_Christian#Bebop_and_Minton.27s_Playhouse "Charlie Christian") of [Charlie Christian](/wiki/Charlie_Christian "Charlie Christian") jamming at [Minton's Playhouse](/wiki/Minton%27s_Playhouse "Minton's Playhouse") around the same time have a sustained snare-drum backbeat on the hottest choruses.

Outside [U.S.](/wiki/United_States "United States") popular music, there are early recordings of music with a distinctive backbeat, such as the 1949 recording of Mangaratiba by [Luiz Gonzaga](/wiki/Luiz_Gonzaga "Luiz Gonzaga") in Brazil.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

}](//upload.wikimedia.org/score/5/q/5q8omctkmm7i2630xe5xx95n81eqr9o/5q8omctk.png)

Delayed backbeat (last eighth note in each measure) as in funk music[[18]](#cite_note-Mattingly-18)

Slap bass executions on the backbeat are found in styles of country western music of the 1930s, and the late 1940s early 1950s music of Hank Williams reflected a return to strong backbeat accentuation as part of the honky tonk style of country.[[19]](#cite_note-19)
In the mid-1940s "[hillbilly](/wiki/Country_music "Country music")" musicians the [Delmore Brothers](/wiki/Delmore_Brothers "Delmore Brothers") were turning out [boogie](/wiki/Boogie "Boogie") tunes with a hard driving back beat, such as the No. 2 hit "Freight Train Boogie" in 1946, as well as in other boogie songs they recorded.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Similarly [Fred Maddox](/wiki/Fred_Maddox "Fred Maddox")'s characteristic backbeat, a [slapping bass](/wiki/Slapping_bass "Slapping bass") style, helped drive a rhythm that came to be known as [rockabilly](/wiki/Rockabilly "Rockabilly"), one of the early forms of [rock and roll](/wiki/Rock_and_roll "Rock and roll").[[20]](#cite_note-20) Maddox had used this style as early as 1937.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

In today's popular music the [snare drum](/wiki/Snare_drum "Snare drum") is typically used to play the backbeat pattern.[[7]](#cite_note-Schroedl-7) Early [funk music](/wiki/Funk_music "Funk music") often delayed one of the backbeats by an eighth note so as "to give a 'kick' to the [overall] beat".[[18]](#cite_note-Mattingly-18)

Some songs, such as [The Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles "The Beatles")' "[Please Please Me](/wiki/Please_Please_Me_(song) "Please Please Me (song)")" and "[I Want to Hold Your Hand](/wiki/I_Want_to_Hold_Your_Hand "I Want to Hold Your Hand")", [The Knack](/wiki/The_Knack "The Knack")'s "[Good Girls Don't](/wiki/Good_Girls_Don%27t "Good Girls Don't")" and [Blondie](/wiki/Blondie_(band) "Blondie (band)")'s cover of [The Nerves](/wiki/The_Nerves "The Nerves")' "[Hanging on the Telephone](/wiki/Hanging_on_the_Telephone "Hanging on the Telephone")", employ a double backbeat pattern.[[22]](#cite_note-wave-22) In a double backbeat, one of the off beats is played as two eighth notes rather than one quarter note.[[22]](#cite_note-wave-22)

Some drummers slightly delay the backbeat on the snare drum to create a more relaxed feel, a technique known as the "delayed" or "late backbeat." In such cases, the intervals between beats 2 and 3, and between beats 4 and 1, are slightly shortened to prevent the tempo from slowing down. This playing style is particularly common in rock and soul music from the late 1960s to the mid-1970s.[[23]](#cite_note-23) Notable examples can be heard in recordings such as "[Green Onions](/wiki/Green_Onions "Green Onions")" by [Booker T. & the M.G.'s](/wiki/Booker_T._%26_the_M.G.%27s "Booker T. & the M.G.'s"), "[And Your Bird Can Sing](/wiki/And_Your_Bird_Can_Sing "And Your Bird Can Sing")" by [The Beatles](/wiki/The_Beatles "The Beatles"), "[Hey Joe](/wiki/Hey_Joe "Hey Joe")" by [The Jimi Hendrix Experience](/wiki/The_Jimi_Hendrix_Experience "The Jimi Hendrix Experience"), and "[Easy](/wiki/Easy_(Commodores_song) "Easy (Commodores song)")" by [The Commodores](/wiki/Commodores "Commodores"). Drummer [Charlie Watts](/wiki/Charlie_Watts "Charlie Watts") of [The Rolling Stones](/wiki/The_Rolling_Stones "The Rolling Stones") frequently employed this technique during this period, as heard in tracks like "[Monkey Man](/wiki/Monkey_Man_(Rolling_Stones_song) "Monkey Man (Rolling Stones song)")," "[Wild Horses](/wiki/Wild_Horses_(Rolling_Stones_song) "Wild Horses (Rolling Stones song)")," "[Sister Morphine](/wiki/Sister_Morphine "Sister Morphine")," and "[Let It Bleed](/wiki/Let_It_Bleed_(song) "Let It Bleed (song)")."[[24]](#cite_note-24) A delayed backbeat can also be heard in the famous eight-bar "[Funky Drummer](/wiki/Funky_Drummer "Funky Drummer")" break, played by [Clyde Stubblefield](/wiki/Clyde_Stubblefield "Clyde Stubblefield").[[25]](#cite_note-25) *Early* backbeats can be heard in [Bruce Springsteen](/wiki/Bruce_Springsteen "Bruce Springsteen")'s "The River," [The Police](/wiki/The_Police "The Police")'s "[Every Breath You Take](/wiki/Every_Breath_You_Take "Every Breath You Take")," and [D'Angelo](/wiki/D%27Angelo "D'Angelo")'s "The Line."[[26]](#cite_note-26)

## Cross-beat

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Beat_(music)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Cross-beat")]

Main article: [Cross-beat](/wiki/Cross-beat "Cross-beat")

> Cross-rhythm. A rhythm in which the regular pattern of accents of the prevailing meter is contradicted by a conflicting pattern and not merely a momentary displacement that leaves the prevailing meter fundamentally unchallenged
>
> :   —*New Harvard Dictionary of Music* (1986: 216).[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-Neal_2000_https://archive.org/details/countrymusicannu00char/page/115_115-28)

## Hyperbeat

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Beat_(music)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Hyperbeat")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Hypermeter.png/330px-Hypermeter.png)](/wiki/File:Hypermeter.png)

Hypermeter: 4 beat measure, 4 measure hypermeasure, and 4 hypermeasure verses. Hyperbeats in red.

A **hyperbeat** is one unit of [hypermeter](/wiki/Hypermeter "Hypermeter"), generally a measure. "Hypermeter is meter, with all its inherent characteristics, at the level where measures act as beats."[[28]](#cite_note-Neal_2000_https://archive.org/details/countrymusicannu00char/page/115_115-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)

## Beat perception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Beat_(music)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Beat perception")]

Beat perception refers to the human ability to extract a periodic time structure from a piece of music.[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31)
This ability is evident in the way people instinctively move their body in time to a musical beat, made possible by a form of [sensorimotor](/wiki/Sensorimotor_network "Sensorimotor network") [synchronization](/wiki/Synchronization "Synchronization") called 'beat-based timing'. This involves identifying the beat of a piece of music and timing the frequency of movements to match it.[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34)
Infants across cultures display a rhythmic [motor response](/wiki/Sensorimotor_network "Sensorimotor network") but it is not until between the ages of 2 years 6 months and 4 years 6 months that they are able to match their movements to the beat of an auditory stimulus.[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36)

## Related concepts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Beat_(music)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Related concepts")]

* *[Tatum](/wiki/Tatum_(music) "Tatum (music)")* refers to a subdivision of a beat which represents the "time division that most highly coincides with note onsets".[[37]](#cite_note-37)
* *Afterbeat* refers to a [percussion](/wiki/Percussion_instrument "Percussion instrument") style where a strong accent is sounded on the second, third and fourth beats of the bar, following the downbeat.[[13]](#cite_note-Grove-13)
* In [reggae](/wiki/Reggae "Reggae") music, the term *[one drop](/wiki/One_drop_rhythm "One drop rhythm")* refers to the complete de-emphasis (to the point of silence) of the first beat in the cycle.
* [James Brown](/wiki/James_Brown "James Brown")'s signature [funk](/wiki/Funk "Funk") groove emphasized the downbeat – that is, with heavy emphasis "on the one" (the first beat of every measure) – to etch his distinctive sound, rather than the back beat (familiar to many R&B musicians) which places the emphasis on the second beat.[[38]](#cite_note-nytimes.com-38)[[39]](#cite_note-fa_maceo-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40)
